title: Seoul's Nuclear Submarine Ambitions Deserve Scrutiny
gist: War on the Rocks' 'The Insider' podcast previews Wilson Grossman-Trawick's article questioning whether South Korea's pursuit of a nuclear-powered submarine is worth its cost and the right capability against North Korean or Chinese threats, rather than a matter of technical feasibility.

insights.claim: War on the Rocks contributor Wilson Grossman-Trawick challenges the prevailing belief within South Korea's political and defense establishment that building a nuclear-powered submarine is worth its cost.
insights.speaker: Wilson Grossman-Trawick
insights.so_what: Flags a live debate over South Korean defense-procurement priorities that could redirect budget away from a nuclear submarine program if the contrarian case gains traction.

insights.claim: Grossman-Trawick questions whether a nuclear-powered submarine is even the right capability to deter and defend against a North Korean or Chinese threat, distinct from the question of whether South Korea has the talent, time, and money to build one.
insights.speaker: Wilson Grossman-Trawick
insights.so_what: Separates the capability-fit question from the feasibility question — a useful framework for evaluating any major weapons-platform investment case, not just Seoul's submarine ambitions.

insights.claim: The article concedes South Korea certainly could achieve building a nuclear submarine, framing the debate around opportunity cost — talent, time, and money — rather than technical feasibility.
insights.speaker: Wilson Grossman-Trawick
insights.so_what: Reframes the South Korean nuclear-submarine debate as a resource-allocation question, relevant for assessing the odds Seoul actually proceeds versus redirecting funds to other capabilities.
insights.quote: certainly could achieve this prestigious feat
